posts.cooked:
Just saw the Rare Wine Co offer trumpeting Pigott’s ratings on Domaine Anita’s 2021s. I’ve seen a few nice notes on earlier vintages, and while I’m no point-chaser and am not expecting “perfection,” I couldn’t resist taking a shot at a 6 pack of mixed bottlings since I always enjoy Moulin a Vent and the prices are still relatively nice. Others experience with Anita? I know Pigott often doles out high scores.

From the email…

On March 4th, one of the most experienced wine writers in the world, Stuart Pigott, gave a slew of eye-popping scores to Domaine Anita, founded by a former competitive cyclist, Anita Kuhnel.

Not only did Pigott hand out several ratings of 96 or higher, for one wine he went where no writer on Beaujolais has ever gone before. He gave the domaine’s 2021 Moulin-a-Vent Coeur de Vigneronne a perfect 100-point rating, with a note to match:

“Imagine gorging yourself on a whole handful of the ripest wild blackberries … On the palate there’s a breathtaking interplay of mineral freshness with a small ocean of berry fruit and super-fine tannins. An endless finish that’s so complex and so vibrant it makes you glad to be alive. Decades of aging potential.”

Moulin-à-Vent Reine de Nuit 97
Moulin-à-Vent Tres Vieille Vigne “Les Caves” 96
Moulin-à-Vent Coeur de Vigneronne 100
Morgon Château-Gaillard 96

Back Story on Domaine Anita
The domaine’s founder, Anita Kuhnel, started her career not in a cellar but as a champion cyclist. But when she retired from cycling in 1996, she returned to her husband André’s childhood home of Beaujolais, where his family has been making wine since 1943.

But first, Anita went to Burgundy to formally train, before joining her husband and then, finally, striking out on her own in 2015. In that year, she took over a 1970s estate in Chénas, which she renamed Domaine Anita.

With more than two decades of experience and a stunning collection of top vineyards in Morgon and Moulin-à-Vent, Anita Kuhnel had the raw materials and the experience to create Beaujolais’ first 100-point wine.

A Note on Stuart Pigott
Pigott is perhaps best known as the man who quite literally wrote the book on German wines (The Wine Atlas of Germany, 1996, forward by Hugh Johnson).

And much like Jancis Robinson’s writing in the Financial Times or Eric Asmov’s in the New York Times, Pigott was for many years the wine columnist for Germany’s third leading newspaper, Frankfurter Allgemeine Zeitung.

Pigott has covered six vintages of Beaujolais for James Suckling. When you add that to his four decades as a professional taster, there can be few more experienced reviewers of this important region.

Reviews of the Wines

2021 Moulin-à-Vent Reine de Nuit
Stuart Pigott: “97 rating … majestic … stunning salty minerality … The very long, super-fine finish makes you wonder why this appellation isn’t as famous as Burgundy’s finest.”

2021 Moulin-à-Vent Tres Vieille Vigne “Les Caves”
Stuart Pigott: “96 rating … Stunning nose … Very elegant and silky … Hard to imagine how a wine could be more focused than this. From 80- and 100-year-old vines.”

2021 Moulin-à-Vent Coeur de Vigneronne
Stuart Pigott: “100 rating … Imagine gorging yourself on a whole handful of the ripest wild blackberries and that is the beginning of this great journey! On the palate there’s a breathtaking interplay of mineral freshness with a small ocean of berry fruit and super-fine tannins. An endless finish that’s so complex and so vibrant it makes you glad to be alive. Decades of aging potential.”

2021 Morgon Château-Gaillard
Stuart Pigott: “96 rating … staggering … The balance of concentration and the super-fine tannins must be tasted to be believed. And the stony minerality at the finish is off the scale …

Background on the Wines

Moulin-a-Vent Reine de Nuit comes from a lieux dit named Aux Caves adjacent to Anita’s cellars. The name refers to the fact that the 40-year-old vines are harvested at night to preserve as much Gamay fruit character as possible. So, expect ravishing fruit and supple texture, but with superb depth and density.

Moulin-a-Vent Tres Vieille Vigne “Les Caves” comes from 80–100-year-old vines planted on a granite hillside. Mineral, driven and focused, this has plenty of aging potential, but will also offer great pleasure if drunk now.

From 60-year-old vines, the Moulin-a-Vent Coeur de Vigneronne is handled like Burgundy in the cellar. The grapes are destemmed, fermented in vat, and allowed to mature slowly in French oak barrels. The results are beguiling, and as Stuart Pigott wrote, it has “decades of aging potential.”

Morgon Château-Gaillard is one of Domaine Anita’s most concentrated wines. Made from 70-year-old vines that produce very small clusters, the wine is quite powerful, with the structure for extended aging.

posts.name: Dennis Atick
posts.cooked: Looking forward to it!

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked: Well, this thread admittedly piqued my curiosity. I drink a ton Beaujolais, get the rare wine emailer, and I love 100 points. Was surprising to me that I have never heard of this estate. So this morning I looked at some notes from Neal Martin, and William Kelley, that admittedly only run up to 2018. My collective interpretation from those notes, however, is that this is a fairly modern winery that likes to express very ripe fruit and uses oak perhaps a bit on the aggressive side. I saw a lot of notes of roast, baked, creamy, vanilla, oak, etc. I am out, not worth the flyer to me.

posts.name: Chris_Rickey
posts.cooked: Not to mention that Neal Martin’s and William Kelley’s notes, while positive, are a far cry from upper-90’s.

posts.name: Morgan S
posts.cooked: Garagiste offered a mixed 4-pack of 2019s as a not black friday mystery. Never was a big Beaujolais drinker but after tried my first one I started seeking out more.

All fair points… and yeah, there’s not a whole lot of good info out there. Best I found was here:

GRAPE

Domaine Anita - GRAPE

Domaine Anita Anita Kuhnel’s first passion in life was professional cycling, that is until her former husband introduced her to her destiny – winemaking. In 2015, she took over 18 hectares of vines first planted in 1973 and created Domaine...

Est. reading time: 9 minutes

The notes on winemaking are reassuring to me, for the Reine de Nuit (no oak at all): Elaboration: Lieux dit Les Caves , located on the village of Chenas, Granit and manganese soils with a South – South/East exposure. Grown at high altitude (240 meters). The Gamay vineyard is 40 years old and the planting density is 10,000 foot/ha. The vines are Gobelet trained, with pruning up to 10 eyes. The vineyard is harvested and sorted manually. Vinification is 100% whole clusters. Cold start pre-fermentation maceration (8 degrees) for 3 days. Total maceration takes 10 days. The end of alcoholic fermentation is at 19 degrees – 20 degrees. The wine is aged 6 months in cement tanks and at least 4 months in bottle before it is released.

And then the one that got 100 points DOES see oak, but I like the notes: Domaine Anita Moulin-à-Vent Coeur de Vigneronne: Elaboration: This wine comes from the village Lieu dit Chenas les Caves that has granit soils and granitic subsoils with a high amount of manganese. The vineyard has an East–South/East exposure. Grown at high altitude (220 meters), the Gamay vineyard is 45 years old and the planting density is 10,000 foot/ha. The vines are Gobelet trained, with pruning up to 10 eyes. The vineyard is harvested and sorted manually. Vinification is 20% whole clusters and 80% destemmed grapes. The high portion of destemmed grapes brings a lot of elegance to the tannins, while increasing the acidity level. Total maceration takes 10 days, with daily cap punching. Pressing in a low pressure horizontal press for slow extraction. The wine is aged 11 months in oak barrels (two to six wines, mostly from Burgundy), and spends 5 months in bottle to bring roundness through micro-oxygenation and counterbalance the higher acidity.
(but micro-oxygenation? really?)

posts.name: Michae1 P0wers
posts.cooked: CT notes seem to reflect a trend for big and modern on these wines as well. This may all be influenced by the recent spate of superripe vintages, but the general tone of the producer, based on the notes from recent wines, seems to be power and density over freshness and finesse. Not my kind of Beaujolais.

posts.name: Jim Cowan
posts.cooked:
bradkaplan:

(but micro-oxygenation? really?)

Just a guess but I think this means that the closure allows some oxygen transport. Especially since it seems to say that bottle aging is responsible for micro-ox.
But the term caught my eye, too.

posts.name: Peter Brush
posts.cooked:
bradkaplan:

micro-oxygenation

Micro-oxygenation is a very specific wine making technique, and isn’t done in the bottle. I wonder if that’s a translation issue?

en.wikipedia.org

Microoxygenation

Micro-oxygenation is a process used in winemaking to introduce oxygen into wine in a controlled manner. Developed in 1991 by Patrick DuCournau, working with the exceptionally tannic grape Tannat in Madiran, the process gained usage in modern winemaking following the 1996 authorization by the European Commission. Today, the technique is widely employed in Bordeaux, as well as at least 11 different countries, including the United States and Chile. The process of micro-oxygenation involves a large ...

posts.cooked: The wines have often been a bit aggressively extracted in my experience, especially this bottling.
Thanks (and sorry to hear that! definitely not what I’m looking for in a Moulin a Vent!).

posts.display_username: Jason L e f l e r

posts.name: Dave Dewhurst
posts.cooked:
I have had quite a few of their wines over the last three or four years since they started to be imported into Oz. never have found any of them them to be oaky or extracted, generally very light and bright, great acidity with pretty classic Beaujolais flavour profiles.

While I haven’t had the 100 pointer, I have had a number of the previous vintages of this specific wine back to 2017 I think, and always found it thoroughly enjoyable. Unfortunately, the 2021 was sold out here in a millisecond, with only very limited allocations compared to previously as a result of the score. To be fair, I would probably have baulked at the price rise from $40 to $70 here, which also dragged some of its sister wines prices higher as well, to $50 as opposed to $35-40 previously.

I would say that the Morgons and Moulin a Vents I have had from this producer are some of their “heavier” wines, but then that is expected for these villages and the inverted commas are very much meaning the heavier end of light-bodied, maybe approaching medium bodied.

Anyway, these wines have very much become a house favourite here but we’ll have to see how pricing and allocations stack up in the future.

Cheers

Dave

posts.name: Jason L e f l e r
posts.cooked: thanks for the perspective dave.
